question_clean: Do you have information about A1C
question_noisy: "Do u have info abotu a1c"
question_repaired: Do you have information about A1C?
reference_answer: Summary : A1C is a blood test for type 2 diabetes and prediabetes. It measures your average blood glucose, or blood sugar, level over the past 3 months. Doctors may use the A1C alone or in combination with other diabetes tests to make a diagnosis. They also use the A1C to see how well you are managing your diabetes. This test is different from the blood sugar checks that people with diabetes do every day. Your A1C test result is given in percentages. The higher the percentage, the higher your blood sugar levels have been: - A normal A1C level is below 5.7 percent - Prediabetes is between 5.7 to 6.4 percent. Having prediabetes is a risk factor for getting type 2 diabetes. People with prediabetes may need retests every year. - Type 2 diabetes is above 6.5 percent - If you have diabetes, you should have the A1C test at least twice a year. The A1C goal for many people with diabetes is below 7. It may be different for you. Ask what your goal should be. If your A1C result is too high, you may need to change your diabetes care plan. NIH: National Institute of Diabetes and Digestive and Kidney Diseases
answer_clean: Yes, I do! A1C is a blood test that measures your average blood glucose levels over the past 2 to 3 months. It can help diagnose diabetes or predict how well someone with diabetes is managing their blood sugar control. The normal range for A1C is less than 5.7%. An A1C level of 6.5% or higher suggests diabetes.
answer_noisy: A1C is short for glycosylated hemoglobin (HbA1c). It is an important blood test that provides information about your average blood glucose levels over the past 2 to 3 months. The normal range of HbA1c is between 4% and 5%. If you have diabetes or other conditions that can affect your blood sugar levels, your doctor may recommend having this test done regularly to monitor how well your treatment plan is working. High HbA1c levels can indicate poor control of your blood sugar, which can lead to serious health problems such as heart disease, kidney damage, blindness, and nerve damage. On the other hand, low HbA1c levels can also be problematic because they can increase the risk of hypoglycemia (low blood sugar), which can cause symptoms such as dizziness, confusion, weakness, seizures, coma, and even death in severe cases.
answer_repaired: Yes, I do! A1C is also known as glycated hemoglobin or HbA1c. It is an important blood test that provides information about your average blood glucose levels over the past 2 to 3 months. The normal range for most people is between 4% (6.0%) and 5.9% (7.8%). If you have diabetes mellitus, it can be used to monitor how well you are controlling your blood sugar with treatment.

question_noisy: I need info on abortions.
question_repaired: "I am seeking information regarding abortion procedures."
reference_answer: An abortion is a procedure to end a pregnancy. It uses medicine or surgery to remove the embryo or fetus and placenta from the uterus. The procedure is done by a licensed health care professional. The decision to end a pregnancy is very personal. If you are thinking of having an abortion, most healthcare providers advise counseling.
answer_clean: An abortion is the intentional termination of a pregnancy before it can be carried to full-term development . This procedure can be performed either through medication or surgery depending on how far along the pregnancy is. Medication abortions typically use a combination of mifepristone and misoprostol to induce an abortion up until 63 days after conception. Surgical abortions include dilation and evacuation procedures such as manual vacuum aspiration (MVA), curettage, hysterectomy, or salpingostomy .
answer_noisy: An abortion is the termination of a pregnancy by removing the fetus or embryo from the uterus before it can survive outside the womb . Abortion is considered one of the safest medical procedures when performed under safe conditions in early pregnancy . In 2018, an estimated 46 million abortions occurred globally, with most taking place in low-resource settings . The World Health Organization (WHO) recommends that all women seeking abortion should be provided with high-quality care to minimize complications and maximize health outcomes .
answer_repaired: Abortion is the intentional termination of a pregnancy by removing fetal tissue from the uterus before 28 weeks' gestation . There are several methods for performing an abortion, including surgical (aspiration curettage or dilation and evacuation) and medical (with medications such as mifepristone and misoprostol). The most common method in the United States is first-trimester aspiration abortion using local anesthesia and sedation . This procedure involves dilating the cervix with forceps and then using suction to remove the embryo and placenta. It typically takes less than 15 minutes to complete and can be performed up until about 13 weeks of pregnancy. Medical abortions involve taking pills that cause chemical miscarriage and can only be done during the first seven weeks of pregnancy.

question_noisy: Rewritten question: what is an abcess [sic], like on the skin?
question_repaired: What is an abscess, and how does it present in the skin?
reference_answer: An abscess is a pocket of pus. You can get an abscess almost anywhere in your body. When an area of your body becomes infected, your body's immune system tries to fight the infection. White blood cells go to the infected area, collect within the damaged tissue, and cause inflammation. During this process, pus forms. Pus is a mixture of living and dead white blood cells, germs, and dead tissue. Bacteria, viruses, parasites and swallowed objects can all lead to abscesses. Skin abscesses are easy to detect. They are red, raised and painful. Abscesses inside your body may not be obvious and can damage organs, including the brain, lungs and others. Treatments include drainage and antibiotics.
answer_clean: An abscess is an infection that forms in tissues or organs within the body, usually due to bacteria. It can occur anywhere in the body but most commonly develops in the skin, lungs, teeth, lymph nodes, appendix, or other areas of the gastrointestinal tract. The pus-filled cavity formed by the infection may be surrounded by inflamed tissue called granulation tissue.
answer_noisy: An abscess is a collection of pus that forms in tissues or organs due to infection. It can occur anywhere in the body but most commonly develops under the armpits, in the groin area, or around hair follicles. Abscesses typically appear as red, swollen areas with a dark center that may be painful when touched. They usually form after a wound becomes infected due to poor hygiene practices such as not washing hands regularly or sharing personal items like razors or toothbrushes. If left untreated, abscesses can spread deeper into surrounding tissue and cause serious complications including sepsis, which is a life-threatening condition. Therefore, it's important to seek medical attention promptly if you suspect you have an abscess.
answer_repaired: An abscess is a collection of pus that forms within tissues or organs due to infection. It can occur anywhere in the body but most commonly develops in the skin, breast tissue, teeth, and internal organs such as the liver, kidney, and spleen. The presentation of an abscess varies depending on its location; however, common symptoms include redness, warmth, swelling, pain, tenderness, and discharge from the site. In some cases, fever may also be present .

question_noisy: What can you tell me about spots?
question_repaired: "What information do you have regarding skin lesions?"
reference_answer: Acne is a common skin disease that causes pimples. Pimples form when hair follicles under your skin clog up. Most pimples form on the face, neck, back, chest, and shoulders. Anyone can get acne, but it is common in teenagers and young adults. It is not serious, but it can cause scars. No one knows exactly what causes acne. Hormone changes, such as those during the teenage years and pregnancy, probably play a role. There are many myths about what causes acne. Chocolate and greasy foods are often blamed, but there is little evidence that foods have much effect on acne in most people. Another common myth is that dirty skin causes acne; however, blackheads and pimples are not caused by dirt. Stress doesn't cause acne, but stress can make it worse. If you have acne - Clean your skin gently - Try not to touch your skin - Avoid the sun Treatments for acne include medicines and creams. NIH: National Institute of Arthritis and Musculoskeletal and Skin Diseases
answer_clean: Acne vulgaris is a common chronic skin condition that affects up to 85% of people during their lifetime . It typically appears in adolescence but can also occur at any age. The exact cause of acne remains unknown; however, it is believed to be related to several factors including genetics, hormones, diet, stress, certain medications, and overgrowth of normal skin bacteria called Propionibacterium acnes .
answer_noisy: Spots refer to small areas of discoloration or pigmentation on the skin that may be visible from birth or develop later in life. They can appear anywhere on the body and range in color from black, brown, gray, blue, pink, red, white, yellow, or orange. answer_repaired: As a medical professional, I can tell you that skin lesion is any change in the normal structure of the skin. It could be a growth or a sore anywhere on your body, including your hair, nails, mouth, genitals, and anus. Skin lesions may appear as birthmarks, moles, spots, bumps, rashes, discolorations, scaly patches, sores, ulcers, lumps, nodules, tumors, or warts. They can also take many different forms such as papule (a small bump), macule (an area of color change without elevation), plaque (flat patch with raised edges), vesicle (fluid-filled sac), blisters, pustule (pus-filled sac), nodule (solid swelling), wheals (red-white hives), erythema (redness of the skin), ecchymosis (bruise) and calluses (thickening of the skin due to repeated friction).

question_noisy: “What is acoustic neuromas? Are they tumors that grow on nerves?”
question_repaired: "What are acoustic neuroma and how do they differ from other types of nerve tumor?"
reference_answer: An acoustic neuroma is a benign tumor that develops on the nerve that connects the ear to the brain. The tumor usually grows slowly. As it grows, it presses against the hearing and balance nerves. At first, you may have no symptoms or mild symptoms. They can include - Loss of hearing on one side - Ringing in ears - Dizziness and balance problems The tumor can also eventually cause numbness or paralysis of the face. If it grows large enough, it can press against the brain, becoming life-threatening. Acoustic neuroma can be difficult to diagnose, because the symptoms are similar to those of middle ear problems. Ear exams, hearing tests, and scans can show if you have it. If the tumor stays small, you may only need to have it checked regularly. If you do need treatment, surgery and radiation are options. If the tumors affect both hearing nerves, it is often because of a genetic disorder called neurofibromatosis. NIH: National Institute on Deafness and Communication Disorders
answer_clean: An acoustic neuroma, also known as vestibular schwannoma or schwannoma of the eighth cranial nerve, is a benign tumor that arises from Schwann cells in the myelin sheath surrounding the vestibulocochlear nerve . It typically presents with unilateral hearing loss, tinnitus, and balance problems. Diagnosis can be made through audiometry, MRI, and surgical biopsy if necessary. Treatment options include observation for small tumors, microsurgical resection to remove the tumor, stereotactic radiosurgery, or chemotherapy .
answer_noisy: Acoustic neuroma (AN) is also known as vestibular schwannoma or neurinoma. It is a benign tumor of Schwann cells in the eighth cranial nerve . ANs can be divided into two types: intrameatal and intracanalicular. Intrameatal ANs occur within the internal auditory canal (IAC), while intracanalicular ANs extend from the IAC to the distal portion of the eighth cranial nerve .
answer_repaired: Acoustic neuromas (ANs) are benign tumors that arise from Schwann cells in the vestibulocochlear nerve . They can be classified as either intrameatal or intracanalicular depending on their location within the internal auditory canal (IAC). Intrameatal ANs originate from the lower part of the IAC near the cochlea, while intracanalicular ANs develop from the upper portion of the IAC near the superior vestibular nerve.

question_noisy: what is acute bronchitis?
question_repaired: "What exactly is acute bronchitis?"
reference_answer: Bronchitis is an inflammation of the bronchial tubes, the airways that carry air to your lungs. It causes a cough that often brings up mucus. It can also cause shortness of breath, wheezing, a low fever, and chest tightness. There are two main types of bronchitis: acute and chronic. Most cases of acute bronchitis get better within several days. But your cough can last for several weeks after the infection is gone. The same viruses that cause colds and the flu often cause acute bronchitis. These viruses spread through the air when people cough, or though physical contact (for example, on unwashed hands). Being exposed to tobacco smoke, air pollution, dusts, vapors, and fumes can also cause acute bronchitis. Less often, bacteria can also cause acute bronchitis. To diagnose acute bronchitis, your health care provider will ask about your symptoms and listen to your breathing. You may also have other tests. Treatments include rest, fluids, and aspirin (for adults) or acetaminophen to treat fever. A humidifier or steam can also help. You may need inhaled medicine to open your airways if you are wheezing. Antibiotics won't help if the cause is viral. You may get antibiotics if the cause is bacterial. NIH: National Heart, Lung, and Blood Institute
answer_clean: Acute bronchitis refers to an inflammation of the airways in the lungs caused by infection with viruses or bacteria . It typically presents as coughing that lasts for less than three weeks, along with other symptoms such as sore throat, runny nose, sneezing, fever, chills, chest congestion, wheezing, shortness of breath, fatigue, and sometimes nausea and vomiting . In most cases, acute bronchitis resolves without treatment within two weeks; however, antibiotics may be prescribed if there is concern about secondary bacterial infection .
answer_noisy: Acute bronchitis refers to an inflammation of the bronchi in the lower respiratory tract caused by viral or bacterial infection. It typically presents with symptoms such as cough (often productive), sore throat, fever, runny nose, wheezing, chest congestion, shortness of breath, and malaise. Symptoms usually last for 7–10 days without treatment but may persist longer if there is a bacterial cause. In most cases, antibiotics are not recommended because it is a self-limiting condition that can be managed at home with rest, fluids, over-the-counter medications for pain relief and symptom management, and close monitoring for signs of worsening. If symptoms do not improve after 3 weeks, a doctor should be consulted.
answer_repaired: Acute bronchitis refers to an inflammation of the airways (bronchi) in the lungs caused by infection with viruses or bacteria . It typically presents as coughing that lasts for 10 days or less without other symptoms such as fever, chills, shortness of breath, wheezing, chest pain, or fatigue. Symptoms may be accompanied by thick mucus production and a low-grade fever. In most cases, antibiotics are not necessary because it is usually caused by a virus rather than a bacterial infection. Treatment involves getting plenty of rest, staying hydrated, over-the-counter medications to help manage symptoms, and avoiding smoking and irritants. Most people recover within one to two weeks. If symptoms persist beyond this time frame, further evaluation should be sought from a healthcare provider due to possible complications.

question_noisy: * typos_grammar: “whut iss Aucyte Lyfomycytic Laekemia”
question_repaired: What is Acute Lymphocytic Leukemia?
reference_answer: Leukemia is cancer of the white blood cells. White blood cells help your body fight infection. Your blood cells form in your bone marrow. In leukemia, however, the bone marrow produces abnormal white blood cells. These cells crowd out the healthy blood cells, making it hard for blood to do its work. In acute lymphocytic leukemia (ALL), also called acute lymphoblastic leukemia, there are too many of specific types of white blood cells called lymphocytes or lymphoblasts. ALL is the most common type of cancer in children. Possible risk factors for ALL include being male, being white, previous chemotherapy treatment, exposure to radiation, and for adults, being older than 70. Symptoms of ALL include: - Weakness or feeling tired - Fever - Easy bruising or bleeding - Bleeding under the skin - Shortness of breath - Weight loss or loss of appetite - Pain in the bones or stomach - Pain or a feeling of fullness below the ribs - Painless lumps in the neck, underarm, stomach, or groin Tests that examine the blood and bone marrow diagnose ALL. Treatments include chemotherapy, radiation therapy, stem cell transplants, and targeted therapy. Targeted therapy uses substances that attack cancer cells without harming normal cells. Once the leukemia is in remission, you need additional treatment to make sure that it does not come back. NIH: National Cancer Institute
answer_clean: Acute lymphocytic leukemia (ALL) is a type of cancer that affects white blood cells called lymphocytes or T-cells. It occurs when there is an abnormal proliferation of immature B-lymphoid precursors in the bone marrow. The disease can be classified into two types based on whether it involves both myeloid and lymphoid lineages (biphenotypic ALL), or only one lineage (monophenotypic ALL).
answer_noisy: Acute lymphocytic leukemia (ALL) is a type of cancer that affects white blood cells called lymphocytes. It can develop in children or adults. In ALL, the lymphocytes do not mature properly and build up in the bone marrow, preventing normal blood cells from being made. This can lead to infection, bleeding, or anemia. The symptoms may include feeling tired, frequent infections, bruising easily, pale skin, swelling in the neck, liver, or spleen, weight loss, pain in the bones, or difficulty breathing. If left untreated, it can be fatal. Diagnosis involves a physical exam, blood tests, bone marrow aspiration, and imaging studies such as CT scans or MRIs. Treatment typically includes chemotherapy followed by stem cell transplantation if necessary.
answer_repaired: Acute lymphocytic leukemia (ALL) is a type of cancer that affects the bone marrow. It occurs when abnormal lymphocytes grow in an uncontrolled way, leading to overproduction of white blood cells. This can cause the bones, liver, spleen, and other organs to become enlarged due to excess cell growth. ALL most commonly develops in children under 15 years old but can also occur in adults . The exact cause of ALL is unknown, but it may be linked to genetic factors or exposure to certain chemicals or radiation. Symptoms include fatigue, fever, weight loss, bruising, bleeding, pain, swelling, and frequent infections. Diagnosis involves a physical exam, blood tests, imaging studies such as X-rays and ultrasound, and a biopsy of the bone marrow. Treatment typically includes chemotherapy, which aims to kill the cancerous cells and induce remission. Depending on the specific subtype of ALL, additional treatments such as stem cell transplantation may be recommended.

question_noisy: Original question: What is an allergy?
question_repaired: Rewritten clear question: Can you explain what allergies are and how they differ from other types of hypersensitivity reactions?
reference_answer: Leukemia is cancer of the white blood cells. White blood cells help your body fight infection. Your blood cells form in your bone marrow. In leukemia, however, the bone marrow produces abnormal white blood cells. These cells crowd out the healthy blood cells, making it hard for blood to do its work. In acute myeloid leukemia (AML), there are too many of a specific type of white blood cell called a myeloblast. AML is the most common type of acute leukemia in adults. This type of cancer usually gets worse quickly if it is not treated. Possible risk factors include smoking, previous chemotherapy treatment, and exposure to radiation. Symptoms of AML include: - Fever - Shortness of breath - Easy bruising or bleeding - Bleeding under the skin - Weakness or feeling tired - Weight loss or loss of appetite Tests that examine the blood and bone marrow diagnose AML. Treatments include chemotherapy, other drugs, radiation therapy, stem cell transplants, and targeted therapy. Targeted therapy uses substances that attack cancer cells without harming normal cells. Once the leukemia is in remission, you need additional treatment to make sure that it does not come back. NIH: National Cancer Institute
answer_clean: Acute myeloid leukemia (AML) is a type of cancer that affects the blood cells in the bone marrow. It occurs when normal precursor cells fail to mature into white blood cells called neutrophils. Instead, these abnormal cells accumulate and crowd out healthy blood cells, leading to an overproduction of immature white blood cells known as blast cells or myeloblasts . AML can occur at any age but is more common in adults aged 65 years or older. The exact cause of AML is unknown, although certain genetic factors may increase the risk of developing this condition. Symptoms typically include fatigue, weight loss, bruising, bleeding, and frequent infections. Treatment options for AML vary depending on the specific subtype of the disease and may involve chemotherapy, radiation therapy, stem cell transplantation, targeted therapies, or supportive care.
answer_noisy: An allergy is an adverse immune response to a substance or stimulus that is harmless to most people. It occurs when the body's immune system overreacts to a specific substance called an allergen. Allergic reactions can be caused by inhaled allergens such as pollen, dust mites, pet dander, mold spores; ingested allergens like certain foods (e.g., peanuts); contact with skin allergens including latex, poison ivy, metals; or exposure to airborne allergens such as insect venom. The symptoms of an allergic reaction range from mild to severe and may include sneezing, itchy eyes, runny nose, hives, swelling, asthma attacks, vomiting, diarrhea, abdominal pain, difficulty breathing, wheeze, cough, chest tightness, low blood pressure, fainting, and even death.
answer_repaired: Allergic reactions occur when an allergen triggers an immune response in the body that is different from a normal reaction to a harmful substance. This type of immune response can cause symptoms such as sneezing, itching, rashes, swelling, or asthma attacks. Other types of hypersensitivity reactions include contact dermatitis (a skin reaction), anaphylaxis (a severe allergic reaction that affects several systems in the body at once), drug hypersensitivity (an abnormal response to certain medications), and delayed-type hypersensitivity (a cellular immune response that occurs 24–72 hours after exposure).

question_noisy: Whut is Addisons disizae ?
question_repaired: What is Addison's disease?
reference_answer: Your adrenal glands are just above your kidneys. The outside layer of these glands makes hormones that help your body respond to stress and regulate your blood pressure and water and salt balance. Addison disease happens if the adrenal glands don't make enough of these hormones. A problem with your immune system usually causes Addison disease. The immune system mistakenly attacks your own tissues, damaging your adrenal glands. Other causes include infections and cancer. Symptoms include - Weight loss - Muscle weakness - Fatigue that gets worse over time - Low blood pressure - Patchy or dark skin Lab tests can confirm that you have Addison disease. If you don't treat it, it can be fatal. You will need to take hormone pills for the rest of your life. If you have Addison disease, you should carry an emergency ID. It should say that you have the disease, list your medicines and say how much you need in an emergency. NIH: National Institute of Diabetes and Digestive and Kidney Diseases
answer_clean: Addison disease, also known as primary adrenocortical insufficiency or hypoadrenalism, is an autoimmune disorder that affects the adrenal glands' cortex, resulting in decreased production of aldosterone and glucocorticoids . It can occur at any age but has a bimodal distribution with peak incidence between 15–30 years old and after 40 years old . The most common cause of Addison disease worldwide is tuberculosis; however, it may be idiopathic in up to half of cases in developed countries .
answer_noisy: Adrenal insufficiency (AI) or Addison's disease is a rare endocrine disorder characterized by chronic adrenocortical deficiencies of mineralocorticoids and glucocorticoids due to damage or destruction of the cortex of both adrenals .
answer_repaired: Addison’s disease (AD) is an autoimmune disorder that affects the adrenal cortex, leading to insufficiency of glucocorticoids and mineralocorticoid hormones . It can occur at any age but usually presents in early childhood or middle-aged adults with symptoms such as fatigue, weight loss, skin hyperpigmentation, low blood pressure, hypoglycemia, and salt cravings . Diagnosis involves measuring plasma renin activity, serum aldosterone levels, urinary steroid metabolites, and ACTH levels . Treatment typically includes lifelong replacement therapy with glucocorticoids and sometimes mineralocorticoids .

question_noisy: What is adhesion?
question_repaired: "What do you mean by 'adhesion'?"
reference_answer: Adhesions are bands of scar-like tissue. Normally, internal tissues and organs have slippery surfaces so they can shift easily as the body moves. Adhesions cause tissues and organs to stick together. They might connect the loops of the intestines to each other, to nearby organs, or to the wall of the abdomen. They can pull sections of the intestines out of place. This may block food from passing through the intestine. Adhesions can occur anywhere in the body. But they often form after surgery on the abdomen. Almost everyone who has surgery on the abdomen gets adhesions. Some adhesions don't cause any problems. But when they partly or completely block the intestines, they cause symptoms such as - Severe abdominal pain or cramping - Vomiting - Bloating - An inability to pass gas - Constipation Adhesions can sometimes cause infertility in women by preventing fertilized eggs from reaching the uterus. No tests are available to detect adhesions. Doctors usually find them during surgery to diagnose other problems. Some adhesions go away by themselves. If they partly block your intestines, a diet low in fiber can allow food to move easily through the affected area. If you have a complete intestinal obstruction, it is life threatening. You should get immediate medical attention and may need surgery. NIH: National Institute of Diabetes and Digestive and Kidney Diseases
answer_clean: An adhesion is an abnormal band of fibrous tissue that connects two tissues together in the body. They can occur anywhere in the body but commonly develop within the peritoneal cavity after surgery or injury to organs such as the bowel.
answer_noisy: Adhesions are bands of scar tissue that form between two organs or surfaces within the abdominal cavity. They can occur after surgery, infection, radiation therapy, or trauma to an organ. These bands of tissue can cause pain, bowel obstruction, infertility, and other complications.
answer_repaired: An adhesion is an abnormal band of fibrous tissue that forms between two surfaces in the body. It can occur as a result of surgery or injury to an organ or tissue, such as the peritoneum (the thin layer of tissue lining inside of the abdominal cavity). Adhesions may also form spontaneously without any prior trauma or surgery. These bands of tissue can cause pain, bleeding, bowel obstruction, infertility, and other complications.

question_noisy: What is adrenal gland cancer?
question_repaired: "What exactly do you mean by 'adrenal gland cancer'?"
reference_answer: Your adrenal, or suprarenal, glands are located on the top of each kidney. Some people have symptoms that come and go. Others have severe, ongoing pain. A diagnosis of AS is based on your medical history and a physical examination. You may also have imaging or blood tests. AS has no cure, but medicines can relieve symptoms and may keep the disease from getting worse. Eating a healthy diet, not smoking, and exercising can also help. In rare cases, you may need surgery to straighten the spine. NIH: National Institute of Arthritis and Musculoskeletal and Skin Disease
answer_clean: Ankylosing spondylitis (AS) is an inflammatory rheumatic disease that primarily affects the axial skeleton, including the sacroiliac joints, vertebrae, and paraspinal ligaments . It can also affect other parts of the body such as the eyes, intestines, and skin. The exact cause of AS is unknown but it appears to be hereditary in some cases. Genetic factors may play a role in determining who develops this condition.
answer_noisy: The disease that can cause your back and spine to become stiff is called osteoarthritis (OA). OA is a degenerative joint disorder characterized by cartilage loss in the joints of the body, leading to bone-on-bone contact between joint surfaces . This condition often occurs in the knees, hips, hands, and lower back, where weight-bearing joints exist. In the case of low back pain caused by OA, it may be accompanied by numbness or tingling sensations along with the leg, known as sciatica .
answer_repaired: Spinal rigidity can be caused by several conditions including infections such as tuberculosis or osteomyelitis, neoplasms (cancerous tumors), inflammatory disorders like ankylosing spondylitis, rheumatoid arthritis, systemic lupus erythematosus, psoriasis, sarcoidosis, trauma to the spine, congenital abnormalities, neurological disorders such as stroke, multiple sclerosis, Parkinson's disease, cerebral palsy, muscular dystrophy, poliomyelitis, myasthenia gravis, and certain medications."

question_noisy: "What is anthrax disease?"
question_repaired: Rewritten clear question: What is the pathophysiology of anthrax infection?
reference_answer: Anthrax is a disease caused by Bacillus anthracis, a germ that lives in soil. Many people know about it from the 2001 bioterror attacks. In the attacks, someone purposely spread anthrax through the U.S. mail. This killed five people and made 22 sick. Anthrax is rare. It affects animals such as cattle, sheep, and goats more often than people. People can get anthrax from contact with infected animals, wool, meat, or hides. It can cause three forms of disease in people. They are - Cutaneous, which affects the skin. People with cuts or open sores can get it if they touch the bacteria. - Inhalation, which affects the lungs. You can get this if you breathe in spores of the bacteria. - Gastrointestinal, which affects the digestive system. You can get it by eating infected meat. Antibiotics often cure anthrax if it is diagnosed early. But many people don't know they have anthrax until it is too late to treat. A vaccine to prevent anthrax is available for people in the military and others at high risk. NIH: National Institute of Allergy and Infectious Diseases
answer_clean: Anthrax is an infectious disease caused by the bacterium Bacillus anthracis. It can occur in three forms: cutaneous, gastrointestinal, or inhalational. Cutaneous anthrax is characterized by one or more skin lesions that begin as small, painless sores that develop into large, ulcerative lesions with black centers. Gastrointestinal anthrax causes symptoms such as fever, abdominal pain, vomiting, and diarrhea. Inhalation anthrax affects the lungs and may be fatal if not treated promptly.
answer_noisy: Anthrax is an infectious disease caused by Bacillus anthracis bacteria that can affect humans and animals . It typically presents as skin lesions or gastrointestinal symptoms in humans after exposure to infected animals or their products (e.g., meat) . The incubation period for anthrax ranges from 2 days to several weeks depending on the route of infection .
answer_repaired: Anthrax is an infectious disease caused by Bacillus anthracis bacteria that can affect humans and animals. The pathogenesis of anthrax involves three distinct stages: (1) Cutaneous stage: This stage occurs when spores enter through cuts or open wounds in the skin. Once inside the body, they begin to germinate into vegetative forms of B. anthracis. These organisms produce two important virulence factors called lethal factor (LF) and edema factor (EF). LF inhibits cell-mediated immunity while EF causes localized swelling at the site of infection. As the rash progresses, it may develop ulcers that ooze black eschar, which is characteristic of this form of anthrax. If left untreated, cutaneous anthrax can spread to other parts of the body.

question_clean: Do you have information about Antibiotic Resistance
question_noisy: What's up with antibiotics?
question_repaired: "What is your understanding of how antibiotics work and what they are used for?"
reference_answer: Summary : Antibiotics are medicines that fight bacterial infections. Used properly, they can save lives. But there is a growing problem of antibiotic resistance. It happens when bacteria change and become able to resist the effects of an antibiotic. Using antibiotics can lead to resistance. Each time you take antibiotics, sensitive bacteria are killed. But resistant germs may be left to grow and multiply. They can spread to other people. They can also cause infections that certain antibiotics cannot cure. Methicillin-resistant Staphylococcus aureus (MRSA) is one example. It causes infections that are resistant to several common antibiotics. To help prevent antibiotic resistance - Don't use antibiotics for viruses like colds or flu. Antibiotics don't work on viruses. - Don't pressure your doctor to give you an antibiotic. - When you take antibiotics, follow the directions carefully. Finish your medicine even if you feel better. If you stop treatment too soon, some bacteria may survive and re-infect you. - Don't save antibiotics for later or use someone else's prescription. Centers for Disease Control and Prevention
answer_clean: Yes, I do! Antibiotics are drugs that kill or inhibit the growth of bacteria. They can also be used to treat other types of infections caused by certain microorganisms such as fungi and protozoa. However, overuse and misuse of antibiotics has led to the emergence of resistant strains of bacteria. This phenomenon is known as "antibiotic resistance" (ABR).
answer_noisy: Antibiotics are medications that fight bacterial infections by killing or inhibiting their growth. They do not work against viruses, fungi, or parasites. It is important to use them responsibly because overuse can lead to resistance among bacteria, making it harder for future treatments to be effective.
answer_repaired: Antibiotics are medications that kill or inhibit bacterial growth by interfering with their metabolic processes such as cell wall synthesis, protein synthesis, DNA replication, and repair mechanisms . They can be classified into several categories including beta-lactams (e.g., penicillin), aminoglycosides (e.g., gentamicin), tetracyclines (e.g., doxycycline), macrolides (e.g., erythromycin), quinolones (e.g., ciprofloxacin) and sulfonamides (e.g., trimethoprim).

question_noisy: do you provide data on antipressents
question_repaired: "Do you have information about prescription medications used for treating high blood pressure?"
reference_answer: Summary : Antidepressants are medicines that treat depression. Your doctor can prescribe them for you. They work to balance some of the natural chemicals in our brains. It may take several weeks for them to help. There are several types of antidepressants. You and your doctor may have to try a few before finding what works best for you. Antidepressants may cause mild side effects that usually do not last long. These may include headache, nausea, sleep problems, restlessness, and sexual problems. Tell your doctor if you have any side effects. You should also let your doctor know if you take any other medicines, vitamins, or herbal supplements. It is important to keep taking your medicines, even if you feel better. Do not stop taking your medicines without talking to your doctor. You often need to stop antidepressants gradually. NIH: National Institute of Mental Health

Some common side effects include nausea, headache, dizziness, sleep problems, anxiety, and sexual dysfunction. It is important for patients to discuss any concerns they may have with their healthcare provider before starting an SSRI medication.

question_clean: Do you have information about Antioxidants
question_noisy: do antioxidant supplements help prevent heart disease
question_repaired: Rewritten clear question: Can taking antioxidants as dietary supplements reduce my risk of developing cardiovascular diseases?
reference_answer: Summary : Antioxidants are man-made or natural substances that may prevent or delay some types of cell damage. Antioxidants are found in many foods, including fruits and vegetables. They are also available as dietary supplements. Examples of antioxidants include - Beta-carotene - Lutein - Lycopene - Selenium - Vitamin A - Vitamin C - Vitamin E Vegetables and fruits are rich sources of antioxidants. There is good evidence that eating a diet with lots of vegetables and fruits is healthy and lowers risks of certain diseases. But it isn't clear whether this is because of the antioxidants, something else in the foods, or other factors. High-dose supplements of antioxidants may be linked to health risks in some cases. For example, high doses of beta-carotene may increase the risk of lung cancer in smokers. High doses of vitamin E may increase risks of prostate cancer and one type of stroke. Antioxidant supplements may also interact with some medicines. To minimize risk, tell you of your health care providers about any antioxidants you use. NIH: National Center for Complementary and Integrative Health
answer_clean: Yes, I do! Antioxidants are molecules that can neutralize free radicals in the body by donating an electron to them. Free radicals are unstable atoms or molecules with one or more unpaired electrons that can cause damage to cells and tissues if not neutralized. Antioxidants play an important role in preventing oxidative stress, which is linked to many chronic diseases such as cancer, heart disease, diabetes, and Alzheimer's disease. There are several types of antioxidants found naturally in foods such as vitamins A, C, E, beta-carotene, lycopene, coenzyme Q10 (CoQ10), glutathione, and polyphenols. Supplemental forms of these antioxidants may be taken in capsule form for their health benefits. However, it is important to note that high doses of some antioxidant supplements may actually increase the risk of certain diseases, so it is always best to consult with a healthcare provider before taking any new supplements.
answer_noisy: Antioxidants may not be effective in preventing cardiovascular diseases (CVD). While some studies have found that high doses of certain antioxidants can reduce CVD risk , others have shown no benefit or even an increased risk . The most recent Cochrane review of randomized controlled trials involving more than 100,000 participants showed that taking vitamin E supplements did not significantly affect the incidence of myocardial infarction, stroke, or death from any cause . Similarly, another study found that beta-carotene supplementation had no effect on the occurrence of coronary artery disease . However, it is important to note that these findings were observed among people who were already at high risk for CVD, so they may not apply to other populations.
answer_repaired: No, there is no evidence that antioxidant supplementation reduces the risk of cardiovascular disease in healthy individuals. In some cases, high doses of certain antioxidants may even increase the risk of adverse health outcomes such as cancer or heart failure.

question_noisy: "What is this anxiety thing that makes people feel worried and nervous?"
question_repaired: What is this condition called where individuals experience worry and nervousness?
reference_answer: Fear and anxiety are part of life. You may feel anxious before you take a test or walk down a dark street. This kind of anxiety is useful - it can make you more alert or careful. It usually ends soon after you are out of the situation that caused it. But for millions of people in the United States, the anxiety does not go away, and gets worse over time. They may have chest pains or nightmares. They may even be afraid to leave home. These people have anxiety disorders. Types include - Panic disorder - Obsessive-compulsive disorder - Post-traumatic stress disorder - Phobias - Generalized anxiety disorder Treatment can involve medicines, therapy or both. NIH: National Institute of Mental Health

While some anxiety is normal in response to stressful situations, excessive or persistent anxiety may indicate an underlying mental health condition such as generalized anxiety disorder (GAD). GAD is a chronic and debilitating illness that causes individuals to experience disproportionate levels of fear and anxiety about everyday events for no apparent reason. The symptoms often interfere with daily activities and quality of life.

answer_repaired: This condition is known as anxiety. Anxiety can be experienced as feelings of tension or apprehension accompanied by physical symptoms such as increased heart rate, sweating, and muscle tension. It may also manifest as avoidance behaviors or excessive preoccupation with potential negative outcomes.

question_noisy: "What do I need to know about an enlarged artery?"
question_repaired: What information should be known regarding an enlarged artery?
reference_answer: An aneurysm is a bulge or "ballooning" in the wall of an artery. Arteries are blood vessels that carry oxygen-rich blood from the heart to other parts of the body. If an aneurysm grows large, it can burst and cause dangerous bleeding or even death. Most aneurysms are in the aorta, the main artery that runs from the heart through the chest and abdomen. There are two types of aortic aneurysm: - Thoracic aortic aneurysms - these occur in the part of the aorta running through the chest - Abdominal aortic aneurysms - these occur in the part of the aorta running through the abdomen Most aneurysms are found during tests done for other reasons. Some people are at high risk for aneurysms. It is important for them to get screening, because aneurysms can develop and become large before causing any symptoms Screening is recommended for people between the ages of 65 and 75 if they have a family history, or if they are men who have smoked. Doctors use imaging tests to find aneurysms. Medicines and surgery are the two main treatments. NIH: National Heart, Lung, and Blood Institute
answer_clean: An aortic aneurysm is a permanent dilation of the aorta that exceeds 50% of its normal diameter . It can occur in any part of the aorta but most commonly affects the abdominal aorta below the chest area or the thoracic aorta above the heart. If left untreated, it may rupture, leading to life-threatening bleeding. The exact cause of aortic aneurysms is unknown; however, certain factors such as smoking, family history, high blood pressure, and genetic conditions like Marfan syndrome increase the risk of developing them. Treatment options include medication management, lifestyle modifications, and surgery depending on the location and size of the aneurysm.
answer_noisy: An enlarged artery can be caused by several factors such as high blood pressure or hypertension, inflammation due to infection, trauma, or injury, genetic conditions, or certain diseases like Kawasaki disease in children. It is important to note that not all causes of enlarged arteries require immediate attention, but it should be evaluated by a healthcare professional for proper diagnosis and treatment plan. If left untreated, severe cases may lead to complications including rupture, dissection, aneurysm formation, stroke, or even death.
answer_repaired: An enlarged artery can indicate various conditions such as hypertension or high blood pressure, atherosclerosis, inflammation of the arteries (arteritis), abnormalities in cholesterol levels, congenital vascular malformations, or other genetic disorders. It is important to note that enlarged arteries may not always signify disease, as certain factors like exercise, pregnancy, and cold temperatures can also cause arterial dilation. The location of the affected artery(s) will determine the potential symptoms, which could range from none to severe pain, numbness, weakness, claudication, organ damage due to decreased blood flow, or even life-threatening complications such as stroke or heart attack. A thorough physical examination, medical history, imaging tests, and laboratory evaluations may help diagnose the underlying condition associated with enlarged arteries.

question_noisy: Rewritten question: Is there any kind of brain problem that can make someone have trouble talking?
question_repaired: What types of neurological conditions may cause difficulties with speaking?
reference_answer: Aphasia is a disorder caused by damage to the parts of the brain that control language. It can make it hard for you to read, write, and say what you mean to say. It is most common in adults who have had a stroke. Brain tumors, infections, injuries, and dementia can also cause it. The type of problem you have and how bad it is depends on which part of your brain is damaged and how much damage there is. There are four main types: - Expressive aphasia - you know what you want to say, but you have trouble saying or writing what you mean - Receptive aphasia - you hear the voice or see the print, but you can't make sense of the words - Anomic aphasia - you have trouble using the correct word for objects, places, or events - Global aphasia - you can't speak, understand speech, read, or write Some people recover from aphasia without treatment. Most, however, need language therapy as soon as possible. NIH: National Institute of Neurological Disorders and Stroke
answer_clean: Aphasia refers to a group of communication disorders that occur after damage or injury to the brain's language centers. It can affect speaking, understanding, reading, writing, and other aspects of language use. The severity of symptoms varies depending on the location and extent of the brain damage.
answer_noisy: Yes, there is a type of brain disorder called primary progressive aphasia (PPA) which causes difficulty in language use without significant impairment in other cognitive domains early in its course . PPA is one of the rare forms of dementia and it affects language function primarily while sparing memory and other cognitive abilities for several years after diagnosis. There are three subtypes of PPA including semantic variant, logopenic variant, and nonfluent variant . The symptoms vary depending on the affected area of the brain but all lead to difficulties with speaking and understanding language.
answer_repaired: There are several neurological disorders that can lead to speech impairment or dysfunction. These include stroke, Parkinson's disease, amyotrophic lateral sclerosis (ALS), multiple sclerosis, cerebral palsy, traumatic brain injury, dementia, epilepsy, and head trauma.
